questions.question_text: In Colorado, if a broker and client have no written agreement specifying the relationship, the default is:
distractors: Single agency for the buyer
Single agency for the seller
Dual agency
questions.explanation: Colorado's default brokerage relationship is transaction-brokerage. Unless the broker and client agree in writing to a different relationship, the broker acts as a transaction-broker.

questions.question_text: A transaction-broker in Colorado owes which duty to both parties?
distractors: Undivided loyalty
Advocacy for one party's negotiating goals
Obedience to all instructions
questions.explanation: A transaction-broker must exercise reasonable skill and care, present all offers timely, account for money, and disclose adverse material facts. A TB owes no loyalty or advocacy to either party, but under C.R.S. 12-10-407(3) may NOT disclose either party's negotiating position or motivations without informed consent.

questions.question_text: A buyer tells a transaction-broker their maximum purchase price. The broker:
distractors: Is not required to keep this confidential
Must disclose it to the seller immediately
Can share it only with the employing broker
questions.explanation: Under C.R.S. 12-10-407(3)(a), a transaction-broker may not disclose, without the informed consent of all parties, that a buyer is willing to pay more than the price offered. Though a TB owes no general fiduciary loyalty, this specific negotiating information is statutorily protected.

questions.question_text: A transaction-broker learns of a material defect affecting a property. The broker must:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose it to the prospective buyer
distractors: Keep it confidential for the seller
Report it to the Commission only
Disclose it only if asked directly
questions.explanation: C.R.S. 12-10-407(2)(b)(VI) requires a transaction-broker to disclose to prospective buyers all adverse material facts actually known, including facts about title, physical condition, defects, and environmental hazards. Disclosures owed to the seller concern the buyer's financial ability to perform and intent to occupy.

questions.question_text: Which fiduciary duty does a transaction-broker in Colorado NOT owe?
distractors: Reasonable skill and care
Accounting for money received
Disclosure of material facts
questions.explanation: Transaction-brokers must provide skill and care, account for money, and disclose material facts, but they do not owe loyalty or advocacy to either party — they assist both neutrally.

questions.question_text: A single agent in Colorado owes full fiduciary duties. These include all EXCEPT:
distractors: Undivided loyalty to their client
Full confidentiality of strategy
Obedience to client instructions
questions.explanation: Single agents owe obedience, loyalty, disclosure, confidentiality, accountability, and reasonable care to their client — not assistance to the opposing party.

questions.question_text: To establish a single-agent relationship in Colorado, the broker must:
distractors: Simply begin working with the client
File a form with the Commission
Have oral consent from the client
questions.explanation: Single agency must be established by written agreement. Since the default is transaction-brokerage, a written agreement is required to change to single agency.

questions.question_text: A seller's single agent learns the buyer is in financial distress. The agent must:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose this to the seller client
distractors: Keep the buyer's situation confidential
Withdraw from the transaction
Report it to the Commission
questions.explanation: A single agent owes a duty of disclosure to their client. Information that could affect the client's interests must be shared, unlike a transaction-broker who has no advocacy obligation.

questions.question_text: Which information may a Colorado transaction-broker disclose WITHOUT the informed consent of the parties?
questions.correct_answer: Adverse material facts about the property's physical condition
distractors: That the buyer is willing to pay more than the offered price
That the seller will accept less than the asking price
The seller's motivation for selling
questions.explanation: C.R.S. 12-10-407(3) prohibits a transaction-broker from disclosing negotiating positions or motivating factors without the informed consent of all parties. By contrast, adverse material facts (title, physical condition, defects, environmental hazards) MUST be disclosed to prospective buyers under 12-10-407(2)(b)(VI).

questions.question_text: A buyer hires a broker as a single agent. The broker receives an instruction to submit a lowball offer. The broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Follow the instruction per the duty of obedience
distractors: Refuse to submit the offer
Submit it only with a higher backup offer
Disclose the buyer's true budget to the seller
questions.explanation: A single agent must perform the terms of the written agreement and promote the client's interests with utmost good faith, loyalty, and fidelity (C.R.S. 12-10-405), which includes presenting the lawful offers the client directs — commonly summarized as 'obedience' on the exam.

questions.question_text: Colorado does not recognize traditional dual agency. Instead, it uses:
distractors: Limited agency
Facilitation brokerage
Intermediary status
questions.explanation: When one brokerage has both the listing and a buyer client, Colorado uses designated brokerage — the employing broker designates separate brokers to work with each party (as single agents or transaction-brokers), avoiding dual agency for the firm (C.R.S. 12-10-403(6)).

questions.question_text: In a designated brokerage arrangement, an employing broker who is not personally designated to either party:
questions.correct_answer: Has no brokerage relationship with either party but retains supervisory duties
distractors: Becomes a dual agent for both parties
Serves as single agent for the seller side
Owes fiduciary duties to both clients
questions.explanation: Under C.R.S. 12-10-403(6)(b), the designated broker's relationship does not extend to the employing broker or the firm. Having designated brokers on both sides does not create dual agency (12-10-403(6)(c)), but the employing broker still must supervise under Commission Rule 6.3.

questions.question_text: A brokerage lists a home and also has a buyer client interested in the same property. The employing broker designates separate brokers as single agents for each party. Each designated broker owes:
distractors: Transaction-broker duties only
Limited advisory duties
No duties beyond disclosure
questions.explanation: Each designated broker owes full single-agent fiduciary duties to their respective client, including loyalty, confidentiality, and disclosure. Designated brokers owe the duties of whichever relationship (single agency or transaction-brokerage) was established in writing with their party.

questions.question_text: In designated brokerage, confidential information from one client:
distractors: Must be shared with the employing broker
Is disclosed to both parties equally
Is reported to the Commission
questions.explanation: Confidential information is kept within each designated broker's relationship. The designated broker for the seller does not share client information with the designated broker for the buyer.

questions.question_text: A small brokerage has only one licensed broker besides the employing broker. Can designated brokerage be used?
questions.correct_answer: Yes — the employing broker may designate themselves for one party
distractors: No — two brokers besides the employing broker are required
No — the buyer must be referred to another brokerage
Yes, but only with Commission pre-approval
questions.explanation: C.R.S. 12-10-403(6)(a) allows the employing broker or any employed broker to be a designated broker. Alternatively, one broker may work with both parties as a transaction-broker for both, but never as a single agent for both (12-10-403(6)(d); Rule 6.7). No Commission approval is involved.

questions.question_text: When must a Colorado broker provide a brokerage disclosure to a buyer or seller?
distractors: Only at the time of contract signing
Within 48 hours of first contact
After the inspection period has ended
questions.explanation: The disclosure must be made before engaging in brokerage activities — Rule 6.5 requires it before eliciting or discussing any confidential information from the consumer — i.e., at the earliest reasonable opportunity, never delayed to contract signing.

questions.question_text: A buyer refuses to sign the brokerage relationship disclosure form. The broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Note it was provided and continue
distractors: Refuse to work with the buyer
Have the buyer sign before any showing
Report the buyer to the Commission
questions.explanation: The disclosure must contain a signature block for acknowledgment, but a signature is not mandatory — if the party declines to sign, the broker notes that fact on a copy of the disclosure and retains it (C.R.S. 12-10-408).

questions.question_text: A client wants to change from transaction-brokerage to single agency mid-transaction. This requires:
questions.correct_answer: A written agreement between the parties
distractors: Commission approval
Consent of the other party in the deal
Starting a new transaction from scratch
questions.explanation: The brokerage relationship can be changed at any time with a written agreement between the broker and client. No Commission approval or third-party consent is needed.

questions.question_text: The brokerage disclosure form explains the types of relationships available. Which relationships must be described?
questions.correct_answer: Seller agency, buyer agency, and transaction-brokerage
distractors: Only the transaction-brokerage relationship
Single agent and traditional dual agency
Buyer agency and seller agency only
questions.explanation: Per C.R.S. 12-10-408, the written disclosure must state that the available relationships include buyer agency, seller agency, and transaction-broker status. Dual agency is not available in Colorado.

questions.question_text: A broker fails to provide the brokerage disclosure to a seller before listing the property. This is:
distractors: Acceptable if disclosed at closing
Only a problem if the seller complains
Permitted for experienced sellers
questions.explanation: The disclosure must be provided at the earliest reasonable opportunity. Failing to provide it before listing is a violation, regardless of the seller's experience level.
